title: After AlexNet: The Deep Learning Takeover
concept: AlexNet's ImageNet win pushed computer vision toward deeper CNNs, large-scale training, and benchmark-driven progress, opening the door to the next wave of architectures such as VGG and GoogLeNet.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What was a big reason AlexNet beat older pipelines?

options.text: It learned feature hierarchies directly from pixels at ImageNet scale
It removed convolution from the network
It used fewer categories than older systems

exercises.explain: Right — AlexNet showed that deep learned features could win on a broad benchmark.

cards.title: One win opened a new road
cards.body: After AlexNet, many researchers stopped asking whether deep CNNs belonged in top-tier vision work. The question shifted to: How do we make them deeper, cleaner, and more efficient?

cards.title: What 'deep learning takeover' means here
cards.body: In computer vision, the deep learning takeover means that strong benchmark results pushed more labs, papers, and engineering effort toward neural networks trained on large datasets with GPUs. AlexNet did not solve vision forever, but it changed where the field put its energy.

cards.title: Traffic after a new bridge opens
cards.body: Imagine a city where everyone uses older roads until one new bridge suddenly makes travel much faster. People do not stay on the old route out of loyalty — they rush to improve and expand the bridge system. AlexNet was that bridge for vision research.

cards.title: What came next
cards.body: After 2012, newer CNN families explored the next questions. VGG asked what happens if you go deeper with a cleaner repeated pattern of small convolutions. GoogLeNet/Inception explored multi-branch efficiency. The field started iterating on deep CNN design much more aggressively.

cards.title: Takeover did not mean 'problem solved'
cards.body: A takeover is not the same as perfection. Deeper models still had challenges: more compute, harder training, and new design tradeoffs. AlexNet changed the direction of travel, not the end of the journey.

cards.title: Before and after AlexNet
cards.body: The biggest shift was what researchers chose to build next.

left.text:
Hand-crafted pipelines still central
Deep CNNs felt less proven
Less urgency around large-scale CNN design

right.text:
Deep CNNs became the main route
Labs pushed depth and scale harder
VGG, GoogLeNet, and others followed

exercises.prompt: Which change best describes the post-AlexNet shift in vision research?

options.text: More researchers focused on improving deep CNNs for large-scale benchmarks
Everyone returned to fixed hand-crafted pipelines as the future
Image classification disappeared as a research topic

exercises.explain: Exactly — AlexNet redirected effort toward deeper learned architectures.

exercises.prompt: True or false: After AlexNet, the field mainly concluded that hand-crafted pipelines were still the best path for broad image recognition benchmarks.
exercises.explain: False — the main shift was toward deep neural networks, not back away from them.

exercises.sentence: After AlexNet, many researchers asked how to build even ___ CNNs.
exercises.explain: Right — one major next question was how to go deeper while keeping the design effective.

exercises.prompt: Which statement best bridges AlexNet to the next architecture family, VGG?

options.text: If AlexNet proved deep CNNs could win, VGG asks how far a cleaner and deeper repeated-convolution design can push that idea
VGG abandoned deep CNNs and returned to manual feature engineering
VGG replaced images with text so depth no longer mattered

exercises.explain: Exactly — VGG is a direct continuation of the 'deeper CNNs work' lesson from AlexNet.

exercises.prompt: A learner says, 'If AlexNet proved deep CNNs could win, what is the natural next question for the field?' Which answer is best?

options.text: How can we build even deeper and cleaner CNNs — like VGG's repeated small-convolution stacks — to push performance further?
How can we avoid neural networks completely and return to fixed descriptors only?
How can we remove benchmarking so no one compares models anymore?
How can we solve image recognition by shrinking every dataset to two classes?

exercises.explain: Exactly. AlexNet opened the deep-learning door; VGG walks through it by asking what a simpler, deeper CNN pattern can do. Unit 113 complete — next up: VGG.
